bytes.topic: Concurrency Model — async/await, goroutines, threads, actors — how each language handles it
bytes.title: C# Async/Await — What the State Machine Is Actually Doing
bytes.body:
Every `async` method in C# gets compiled into a state machine by the compiler. When you `await` an incomplete `Task`, the method suspends, returns control to the caller, and resumes on a captured `SynchronizationContext` (or `ThreadPool` if none exists). This is cooperative, not preemptive — no goroutine scheduler, no OS thread per coroutine.

Unlike Go's goroutines (M:N green threads with a runtime scheduler) or Java's virtual threads, C# async/await is purely callback-based under the hood — the `IAsyncStateMachine` drives the continuations manually. No thread is blocked during an `await` on I/O; the `ThreadPool` thread is released and reassigned.

For CPU-bound parallelism, `Task.Run()` offloads to the `ThreadPool`. For actor-like patterns, `System.Threading.Channels` gives you typed, backpressured message passing without a framework. `Parallel.ForEachAsync` (introduced in .NET 6) handles bounded concurrency over async workloads cleanly. Avoid `async void` outside event handlers — exceptions escape the `Task` graph and crash the process silently.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — exceptions vs Result types, error propagation patterns per language
bytes.title: C# Error Handling — Exceptions vs Result Types and When to Use Each
bytes.body:
C# exceptions are expensive: each throw allocates a stack trace and unwinds the call stack. For expected failure paths — validation, not-found, parse errors — this is the wrong tool. The Result<T> pattern (popularized by ErrorOr, FluentResults, or hand-rolled) makes failure a first-class return value, forcing callers to handle it at compile time.

Use exceptions for truly exceptional conditions: unrecoverable I/O failures, violated invariants, programmer errors. Use Result<T> for business logic that regularly fails: a user lookup that returns NotFound, a payment that gets declined.

Error propagation in C# is manual with Result — you chain .Map(), .Bind(), or use switch expressions. With OneOf or ErrorOr, you get discriminated union semantics without the boilerplate. The key discipline: never let a Result silently drop its error. Treat .Value access without checking IsError the same way you'd treat a null dereference — a bug.

For async pipelines, Result composes cleanly with Task<Result<T>>, avoiding exception-based control flow across await boundaries.

bytes.topic: Memory Management — GC vs ownership model, stack vs heap, memory safety
bytes.title: C# Memory Management — GC, Spans, and Why You Don't Always Need the Heap
bytes.body: C#'s GC is a tracing, generational collector (Gen0/1/2 + LOH). Most allocations land in Gen0 and are collected cheaply, but pressure mounts fast with short-lived reference types. The key insight: stack allocations never touch the GC at all. `stackalloc` gives you a raw block on the stack; `Span<T>` wraps it safely with bounds checking. For struct types, value semantics mean copies, not references — no heap, no GC involvement, no pointer indirection. C# has no ownership model (unlike Rust), but `ref struct` enforces stack-only lifetime at compile time — the compiler blocks boxing or storing it on the heap. For large, temporary buffers, `ArrayPool<T>.Shared.Rent()` recycles heap arrays, slashing Gen0 pressure. `IDisposable` + `using` handles deterministic release of unmanaged resources (file handles, native memory via `NativeMemory.Alloc`). The GC handles managed memory; you own the unmanaged side entirely. Understanding this boundary — managed vs. unmanaged, stack vs. heap, GC-tracked vs. pooled — is where C# performance work actually happens.

bytes.topic: Type Safety — static vs dynamic typing, type inference, generics across languages
bytes.title: C# Type Safety — Static Typing, var Inference, and Generics Done Right
bytes.body: C# is statically typed at compile time, but var and dynamic let you trade off strictness deliberately. var is purely compile-time inference — the compiler resolves the concrete type immediately, so you get full IntelliSense and zero runtime cost. dynamic defers binding entirely to runtime via the DLR, bypassing the type system; it's useful for COM interop or deserializing unknown JSON shapes, but every member access becomes a runtime gamble. Generics are where C# type safety compounds. Unlike Java's type-erased generics, C# preserves full generic type information at runtime via reification — List<int> and List<string> are distinct types in the CLR. This enables typeof(T), constraints like where T : IComparable<T>, and covariance/contravariance on interfaces (IEnumerable<out T>, Action<in T>). The practical win: a method constrained to where T : struct, INumber<T> (introduced in .NET 7 via generic math) enforces numeric semantics at compile time, eliminating entire categories of runtime exceptions without reflection or boxing.

bytes.topic: Package Management — lock files, versioning, dependency auditing
bytes.title: C# NuGet Lock Files — Pinning, Versioning, and Auditing Dependencies
bytes.body:
NuGet's lock file (`packages.lock.json`) gives you reproducible restores across machines and CI. Enable it per project with `<RestorePackagesWithLockFile>true</RestorePackagesWithLockFile>` in your `.csproj`, or globally via `Directory.Build.props`. Once generated, pass `--locked-mode` to `dotnet restore` in CI to fail fast if the lock file is stale — no silent version drift.

For version ranges, prefer `[1.2.3]` (exact pin) for third-party libs with breaking-change history, or `1.*` for internal packages you control. Avoid open-ended `*` in production .csproj files; it opts you into unreviewed updates on every restore.

For auditing, `dotnet list package --vulnerable` queries the NuGet vulnerability database and surfaces CVEs with severity levels. Pipe it into CI with `--include-transitive` to catch indirect threats. Pair this with Central Package Management (`Directory.Packages.props`) so version pins live in one file — a single PR changes a version across every project in a solution, and audits target one source of truth.
bytes.code_snippet:
<PropertyGroup>
  <!-- Enable lock file -->
  <RestorePackagesWithLockFile>true</RestorePackagesWithLockFile>
</PropertyGroup>

<!-- Directory.Packages.props (Central Package Management) -->
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>true</ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageVersion Include="Dapper" Version="[2.1.35]" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Npgsql" Version="8.*" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>

<!-- CI: fail if lock file is out of date -->
<!-- dotnet restore --locked-mode -->
<!-- dotnet list package --vulnerable --include-transitive -->

bytes.topic: Testing — unit testing philosophy and test runner per language
bytes.title: C# Unit Testing — xUnit, AAA Pattern, and the Philosophy Behind Isolated Tests
bytes.body:
C# unit tests live in a separate class library project referencing xUnit, Moq, and FluentAssertions via NuGet. Each test method carries a [Fact] attribute for single-path assertions or [Theory] + [InlineData] for parameterized cases. The Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) pattern is the enforced convention: set up dependencies, invoke the SUT, assert outcomes — no mixing concerns.

The core philosophy is isolation: a unit test must never touch the database, filesystem, or network. Dependencies like IRepository or IEmailService are injected via constructor and swapped with Mock<T> objects. Use mock.Setup(x => x.Method()).Returns(value) to define behavior and mock.Verify() to assert interactions.

Avoid testing implementation details — test observable behavior through the public API. One assertion per test keeps failures diagnostic. FluentAssertions makes this readable: result.Should().BeEquivalentTo(expected). The dotnet test CLI runs the full suite; coverage is measured via coverlet. Keep tests fast (sub-millisecond), deterministic, and independent — any test should pass in isolation or in any order.

bytes.topic: Performance — benchmarking, profiling, runtime characteristics per language
bytes.title: C# Benchmarking with BenchmarkDotNet — Beyond Stopwatch
bytes.body:
Stopwatch-based timing is unreliable for micro-benchmarks in C#. The JIT warm-up phase, GC collections, and CPU branch prediction all skew naive measurements. BenchmarkDotNet eliminates this noise by running multiple iterations, controlling JIT compilation, and reporting allocations alongside throughput.

Decorate methods with [Benchmark] and run via BenchmarkRunner.Run<T>(). Add [MemoryDiagnoser] to capture heap allocations per operation — critical when comparing LINQ chains against manual loops, or string interpolation against StringBuilder. Use [Params] to test across input sizes and surface O(n) regressions automatically.

For runtime profiling beyond benchmarks, dotnet-trace captures ETW/EventPipe events without attaching a debugger. Combine with dotnet-counters to watch GC heap size, threadpool queue depth, and exception rates live in production. PerfView remains the gold standard for CPU flame graphs and GC allocation stacks on Windows. The [DisassemblyDiagnoser] attribute in BenchmarkDotNet lets you inspect JIT-emitted assembly directly — indispensable when verifying that Span<T> optimizations or AggressiveInlining hints actually take effect.

bytes.topic: Standard Library — what's built in vs what needs a third-party library
bytes.title: C# Standard Library — What .NET Gives You Free vs What Needs a NuGet Package
bytes.body:
The .NET BCL covers more ground than most developers realize, which means unnecessary NuGet dependencies sneak into projects.

Built-in and often overlooked: `System.Text.Json` (fast, AOT-safe JSON since .NET Core 3.1), `HttpClient` with `IHttpClientFactory`, `System.Security.Cryptography` (AES, RSA, HMACSHA256), `Channel<T>` for producer-consumer pipelines, `System.Threading.RateLimiting` (token bucket, sliding window — .NET 7+), and `Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection` plus `ILogger<T>` as first-class citizens.

Still legitimately third-party: **Polly** for retry/circuit-breaker policies beyond the basic `Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience` wrapper, **FluentValidation** for rule-based validation (`DataAnnotations` won't scale), **Mapster** or **AutoMapper** for object mapping (no built-in mapper exists), **CsvHelper** for CSV (no BCL equivalent), and **Serilog**/**NLog** for structured log sinks — `ILogger<T>` is just an abstraction.

The trap: reaching for `Newtonsoft.Json` by habit. `System.Text.Json` handles 95% of cases. Use `JsonSerializerOptions` with `ReferenceHandler.Preserve` or source generators before adding 500KB of Json.NET.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — REPL, debugger, language server protocol support
bytes.title: C# Dev Tooling — REPL, Debugger, and LSP Support You Should Actually Be Using
bytes.body:
C# Interactive (csi.exe) ships with the .NET SDK and gives you a full REPL with access to NuGet packages via #r "nuget:Newtonsoft.Json". It supports multi-line expressions, top-level statements, and full IntelliSense when used inside Visual Studio's C# Interactive window. For scripting, .csx files run via dotnet-script give you the same experience with a shebang line.

The C# language server is Roslyn-backed OmniSharp or the newer roslyn LSP server (Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.LanguageServer), which powers VS Code's C# Dev Kit. The LSP server exposes go-to-definition, find-all-references, rename, and real-time diagnostics via the standard LSP protocol — meaning any LSP-compatible editor (Neovim, Helix, Zed) gets first-class C# support.

For debugging outside Visual Studio, use netcoredbg with the DAP (Debug Adapter Protocol) — it integrates with VS Code's launch.json and supports conditional breakpoints, expression evaluation, and step-into for async state machines. Pair it with MADGE or dotnet-trace for sampling CPU profiles without attaching a full debugger.

bytes.topic: Security — memory safety, injection prevention, dependency vulnerability scanning
bytes.title: C# Security — Memory Safety, Injection Prevention, and Dependency Scanning
bytes.body:
C# is memory-safe by default — the GC-managed heap prevents buffer overflows. But `unsafe` blocks and `stackalloc` reintroduce those risks. Every `fixed` pointer dereference and manual `MemoryMarshal.CreateSpan` call bypasses runtime bounds checks; audit them carefully. Prefer `Span<T>` and `Memory<T>` for stack-local slices — they enforce bounds by default.

SQL injection most commonly enters through EF Core's `FromSqlRaw`, which accepts raw string concatenation. Use `FromSqlInterpolated` instead — it wraps interpolated values in `DbParameter` automatically, never embedding them as literals. For Dapper, always use anonymous-object parameters, never string concatenation.

For dependency vulnerabilities, `dotnet list package --vulnerable --include-transitive` queries the NuGet vulnerability database (backed by GitHub Advisory). Transitive dependencies are where CVEs hide — the `--include-transitive` flag is non-negotiable. The command returns a non-zero exit code on findings, making it CI-compatible out of the box. Pair it with `dotnet outdated` (global tool) to surface unmaintained packages before they become attack vectors.

bytes.topic: Runtime & Execution — compiled vs interpreted vs JIT, startup time, cold start
bytes.title: C# JIT vs NativeAOT — Cold Starts, Startup Time, and When to Use Each
bytes.body:
C# compiles to CIL (Common Intermediate Language), not native machine code. At runtime, RyuJIT translates CIL to native instructions on first execution. Startup involves two phases: assembly loading and JIT-compiling hot paths. Tiered compilation (default since .NET Core 3.0) mitigates this by generating fast Tier 0 code initially, then recompiling hot methods as optimized Tier 1 in the background.

For Azure Functions or CLI tools, JIT cold starts are real — .NET 8 functions can take 1–3 seconds cold. Two alternatives exist:

**ReadyToRun (R2R):** Pre-compiles IL to native at publish time, retaining a CIL fallback. Reduces JIT overhead ~30–40% but still requires the CLR. Safe for apps using reflection.

**NativeAOT (.NET 7+):** Produces a fully self-contained native binary. Startup drops below 10ms, no JIT, no CLR — but dynamic reflection, `Assembly.Load`, and `Activator.CreateInstance` are restricted or unavailable.

Use R2R for ASP.NET Core APIs where reflection-heavy middleware (EF Core, Swagger) is in play. Use NativeAOT for CLIs, serverless lambdas, or latency-sensitive edge workloads where cold start budget is under 50ms.

bytes.topic: Deployment — binary size, containerization, runtime dependencies
bytes.title: C# Deployment — Shrinking Binaries, Containers, and Runtime Dependencies
bytes.body:
C# gives you three deployment modes that dramatically affect image size and portability. Framework-dependent deployments (the default) produce tiny outputs (~1 MB) but require the .NET runtime on the host — fine for controlled environments, fragile elsewhere. Self-contained deployments bundle the runtime, ballooning output to ~70 MB, but eliminate the host dependency entirely. The sweet spot is combining `--self-contained true` with `PublishSingleFile` and IL trimming via `PublishTrimmed`, which can cut self-contained binaries down to ~20 MB by stripping unused assemblies.

In Docker, always use multi-stage builds: build in `mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk`, copy artifacts into `mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet` (framework-dependent) or `mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/runtime-deps` (self-contained + trimmed). The `runtime-deps` image is ~13 MB vs `aspnet` at ~220 MB. ReadyToRun (`PublishReadyToRun=true`) pre-JITs IL to native code, reducing cold-start latency at the cost of ~20% larger output — worth it for containerized APIs with frequent scale-out. Always pin image tags to digest hashes in production, not `latest`.

bytes.topic: Language-specific — Node.js event loop, Python GIL, Go channels, Rust ownership, Elixir OTP
bytes.title: C# Task Internals — How the Thread Pool and async/await Replace a GIL
bytes.body:
C# has no Global Interpreter Lock, no event loop, and no single-threaded runtime constraint. Instead, the CLR's thread pool combined with async/await gives you cooperative, non-blocking concurrency on top of true OS-level parallelism.

When you await an I/O-bound Task, the calling thread is returned to the pool immediately — no thread is blocked. The continuation resumes on whichever pool thread is available, coordinated by SynchronizationContext (or none, in ASP.NET Core). This is fundamentally different from Node.js, where a single thread processes the event queue, or Python, where the GIL serializes CPU-bound threads.

For CPU-bound work, Parallel.ForEachAsync and Task.Run actually run on separate threads in parallel — no GIL bottleneck. Channel<T> (System.Threading.Channels) gives you Go-channel-style producer/consumer pipelines with backpressure, fully integrated into async/await. Unlike Go's goroutine scheduler, you're working with real OS threads managed by the CLR's hill-climbing algorithm, which tunes pool size at runtime based on throughput.

bytes.topic: Scalability — horizontal scaling characteristics, thread model implications
bytes.title: C# Horizontal Scaling — What the ThreadPool and async/await Actually Mean for Your Architecture
bytes.body:
ASP.NET Core scales horizontally well, but only if you understand what's happening under the hood. Kestrel uses a small pool of I/O threads paired with the CLR ThreadPool. When a request awaits an async I/O operation — `await dbContext.SaveChangesAsync()`, `await httpClient.GetAsync(...)` — the thread is returned to the pool, not blocked. This means 10 ThreadPool threads can serve thousands of concurrent requests, making stateless pods behind a load balancer cheap to scale.

The trap: mixing sync-over-async (`Task.Result`, `.GetAwaiter().GetResult()`) blocks ThreadPool threads, causing starvation under load. With 8 CPUs, you exhaust threads fast. Use `ConfigureAwait(false)` in library code to avoid deadlocks on SynchronizationContext.

For true horizontal scale, enforce statelessness: move session to Redis (`IDistributedCache`), avoid in-process singletons that hold mutable state, and use `IHttpContextAccessor` carefully across async boundaries. Sticky sessions are a scaling anti-pattern — if your pods aren't interchangeable, adding more doesn't help. `CancellationToken` propagation across all async calls ensures graceful pod shutdown during rolling deploys.

bytes.topic: Ecosystem Maturity — library quality, community size, long-term viability
bytes.title: C#'s Ecosystem Maturity — Why NuGet's 350K Packages Are Only Part of the Story
bytes.body:
C#'s ecosystem isn't just large — it's institutionally backed. Microsoft ships first-party libraries for nearly every backend concern: `Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection`, `Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore`, `System.Text.Json`, and `Microsoft.Extensions.Http` (typed `HttpClient` with Polly-style resilience via .NET 8's `AddStandardResilienceHandler`). These aren't community afterthoughts — they're versioned alongside the runtime and carry LTS guarantees.

For cross-cutting concerns, the community fills gaps with high-quality, stable libraries: Dapper (lightweight SQL mapping, 17M+ monthly downloads), MediatR (in-process CQRS), FluentValidation, Serilog, and AutoMapper all follow semantic versioning and maintain changelogs. NuGet's package signing and `dotnet list package --vulnerable` integrate directly into CI to catch deprecated or CVE-flagged dependencies.

Long-term viability is anchored by the .NET Foundation and Microsoft's public roadmap. .NET 8 is an LTS release (supported until November 2026), giving teams a stable target. The `global.json` file pins SDK versions per repo, eliminating 'works on my machine' drift across teams. C#'s ecosystem maturity means fewer abandoned dependencies and more predictable upgrade paths than most ecosystems.

bytes.topic: When to Choose — use cases where each language excels vs where it struggles
bytes.title: C# — When It Dominates and Where It Falls Short
bytes.body: C# excels in enterprise backend systems, real-time applications, and cross-platform desktop/mobile via .NET MAUI. Its strong type system, async/await model, and LINQ make it exceptional for data-heavy APIs, financial services, and game development via Unity. ASP.NET Core Minimal APIs deliver benchmark-leading throughput — often outperforming Go in JSON serialization benchmarks on TechEmpower. Source generators and AOT compilation via NativeAOT close the startup-time gap against Go and Rust for serverless workloads. However, C# struggles where it matters: cold-start latency in AWS Lambda (without NativeAOT) can hit 1–3 seconds, making it a poor fit for high-frequency serverless triggers. Systems programming requiring manual memory control (device drivers, OS kernels) are non-starters — Rust or C++ own that space. Scripting and data science workflows feel unnatural; Python's ecosystem dominates there. If your workload is long-running, stateful, or enterprise-grade, C# is a top-tier choice. For ephemeral, memory-constrained, or shell-automation tasks, reach for something else.

bytes.topic: Code Quality — linting, formatting, language-specific idioms
bytes.title: C# Roslyn Analyzers — Enforce Idioms Without Code Reviews
bytes.body: Roslyn analyzers enforce C#-specific idioms at compile time — no third-party linter needed. Drop a `.editorconfig` at your solution root and configure analyzer severity per rule. The highest-impact rules: `dotnet_style_prefer_is_null_check_over_reference_equality_method` enforces `is null` over `== null`, avoiding operator overload traps; `csharp_prefer_simple_using_statement` replaces `using` blocks with declarations (C# 8+); `dotnet_style_require_accessibility_modifiers` mandates explicit access modifiers. Set rules to `:error` to block CI merges, `:warning` for gradual adoption. Run `dotnet format --verify-no-changes` in CI to enforce formatting without side effects. StyleCop.Analyzers layers naming conventions and XML doc requirements on top. For suppressions, prefer `#pragma warning disable IDE0041 // <reason>` over `[SuppressMessage]` — it shows up clearly in diffs. Add `<AnalysisMode>All</AnalysisMode>` to your `.csproj` to opt into all CA*, IDE*, and SYSLIB* diagnostics. Start with `Recommended`, triage the warnings, then escalate critical rules to `:error`.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — runtime metrics, profiling in production, GC pauses
bytes.title: C# Production Monitoring — GC Pauses, Runtime Metrics, and EventPipe Profiling
bytes.body:
Since .NET 8, the runtime automatically publishes metrics under the `System.Runtime` meter — heap size, GC pause ratio, threadpool queue length, exception count. Wire them into OpenTelemetry with a single `AddRuntimeInstrumentation()` call and they flow to Prometheus or Azure Monitor with zero custom code.

For GC pauses specifically, `GC.GetGCMemoryInfo()` returns `PauseTimePercentage` and per-generation pause durations after each collection — useful for alerting. When you need finer granularity, subscribe to `Microsoft-Windows-DotNETRuntime` via `EventListener` with the GC keyword (`0x1`) to capture `GCSuspendEEBegin`/`GCSuspendEEEnd` events and compute exact stop-the-world durations inline.

For production profiling without restart, `dotnet-trace collect --process-id <pid> --profile gc-verbose` streams ETW/EventPipe data to a `.nettrace` file you open in PerfView or SpeedScope. Pair with `dotnet-counters monitor --counters System.Runtime` for a live terminal dashboard. If Gen2 collection frequency spikes, check for LOH pressure — objects ≥85,000 bytes always land there and never compact by default unless you call `GCSettings.LargeObjectHeapCompactionMode = GCLargeObjectHeapCompactionMode.CompactOnce`.
